content:
# Hebrews Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.9 — "a sabbath rest" vs. "a special rest"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there remains a sabbath rest for the people of God" | sabbatismos |
| LOGOS | "there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "there is a special rest still waiting for the people of God" | "Special rest" for sabbatismos; adds "still waiting" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. sabbatismos is a specific term — "sabbath-keeping/sabbath rest." It is the only occurrence in the NT and is deliberately distinguished from katapausis ("rest") used throughout the chapter. PNEUMA's "special rest" loses the sabbath connection entirely. The author's point is that the eschatological rest is sabbath-shaped — patterned on God's seventh-day rest.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA removes the sabbath reference.

---

### v.12 — "sharper than every two-edged sword" vs. "sharper than the sharpest two-edged sword"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sharper than every two-edged sword" | tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomon |
| LOGOS | "sharper than any two-edged sword" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "sharper than the sharpest two-edged sword" | Superlative for comparative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek is comparative: "sharper than any/every two-edged sword." PNEUMA changes to superlative ("the sharpest"), which shifts the image. The author says God's word exceeds every sword — it is in a different category. PNEUMA's superlative keeps it within the sword category (the sharpest of them).

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.12 — "discerning the thoughts and intentions" vs. "exposes our innermost thoughts and desires"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "able to judge the thoughts and intentions of the heart" | kritikos enthumēseōn kai ennoiōn kardias |
| LOGOS | "discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "It exposes our innermost thoughts and desires" | "Exposes" for kritikos; "desires" for ennoiōn; adds "innermost" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kritikos means "able to judge/discern" — the word is a judge, not merely an exposer. PNEUMA's "exposes" reduces the judicial function to revelation. Also, ennoiai means "intentions/thoughts," not "desires" — PNEUMA shifts from cognitive to appetitive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA reduces judgment to exposure and shifts intentions to desires.

---

### v.15 — "tempted in all things according to our likeness" vs. "faced all of the same testings we do"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "tested in all things according to our likeness, apart from sin" | pepeirasmenon kata panta kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias |
| LOGOS | "one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he faced all of the same testings we do, yet he did not sin" | "Faced" for pepeirasmenon; "testings" for peirasmos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. All three convey the core meaning. PNEUMA's "faced all of the same testings we do" is adequate, though "faced" is slightly less specific than "tempted/tested" (pepeirasmenon). The key phrase chōris hamartias ("without/apart from sin") is preserved by all three.

**Status:** Minor. All three are acceptable.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA removes technical terms | 1 | "Special rest" for sabbatismos (v.9) |
| PNEUMA reduces judicial language | 1 | "Exposes" for "discerns/judges" (v.12) |
| PNEUMA shifts word categories | 1 | Superlative for comparative (v.12) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most significant disagreement is v.9 — the loss of sabbatismos. This is a hapax legomenon that the author chose precisely because it carries the sabbath connotation that katapausis does not. PNEUMA's "special rest" erases the sabbath theology entirely. The author's argument depends on the connection between God's seventh-day rest (v.4), the promised rest (vv.1-8), and the sabbath rest (v.9) — three facets of one reality.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
